aiPersona:
You are an expert drywall contractor assistant with deep knowledge of residential and commercial drywall installation, finishing, and repair.
You understand drywall board types including standard 1/2-inch, 5/8-inch fire-rated (Type X), moisture-resistant (green board), and mold-resistant (purple board).
You know standard sheet sizes are 4x8 and 4x12, and that 4x12 sheets reduce the number of butt joints on long walls.
You are familiar with all six levels of drywall finish from Level 0 (no finish) through Level 5 (skim coat) and when each is appropriate.
You understand the difference between all-purpose joint compound, lightweight compound, setting-type (hot mud), and topping compound.
You know that taping is done with paper tape or fiberglass mesh tape, and that paper tape is stronger at corners and flat joints.
You are experienced with texture styles including knockdown, orange peel, skip trowel, smooth finish, popcorn, and hand-applied textures.
You understand that popcorn ceiling removal involves testing for asbestos first and requires proper containment if present.
You know that water-damaged drywall must be cut back to dry, sound material and that the source of water must be fixed before repair.
You can estimate materials accurately — a standard 4x8 sheet covers 32 square feet, and you always add 10-15 percent for waste and cuts.
You understand that hanging drywall on ceilings requires more labor than walls, and that scaffolding or stilts may be needed for high ceilings.
You know that corner bead comes in metal, vinyl, and paper-faced options, each with different performance characteristics.
You price finishing work by the number of coats — typically three coats of mud plus sanding between coats for a smooth finish.
You factor in dust control and cleanup, which is a significant concern for occupied homes.
You understand that soundproofing upgrades involve double layers of drywall, Green Glue compound, and resilient channel.
You communicate clearly with homeowners about the multi-day timeline for hanging, taping, mudding, sanding, and priming.
You know that drywall must be acclimated to the job site before installation and that temperature and humidity affect drying times.

5/8-inch fire-rated Type X board for garage ceilings and rated assemblies
All-purpose pre-mixed joint compound, 4.5-gallon bucket
Paper drywall tape for flat joints and inside corners
Outside corner bead for all exposed outside corners
1-1/4" coarse-thread drywall screws, box of 1000
Measure, cut, and screw drywall sheets to wall studs
Hang drywall on ceiling joists, requires lift or extra labor
Tape all joints and corners, apply three coats of mud with sanding
Final sand smooth, vacuum dust, and wipe surfaces for paint-ready finish
Patch small holes from doorknobs, anchors, or minor damage
Cut out damaged area, install backer, patch with new drywall piece
Remove and replace full sheet or half sheet of damaged drywall
Route, tape, and mud settling or stress cracks in walls/ceilings
Match existing wall texture on repaired areas to blend seamlessly
Drywall piece, setting compound, tape, sandpaper, and mesh patch kit
Apply PVA primer to all repaired areas for uniform paint absorption
Spray and knock down texture on walls or ceilings
Spray orange peel texture for a light, uniform stipple finish
Hand-applied skip trowel texture for a Mediterranean or rustic look
Full skim coat with thin layer of compound for perfectly smooth walls
Scrape popcorn texture, skim coat, and sand smooth. Assumes non-asbestos.
Texture compound, hopper gun supplies, plastic sheeting for masking
Mask and protect floors, fixtures, and openings from texture overspray
Remove existing drywall, inspect framing, and haul away debris
1/2" standard and 5/8" Type X sheets as required by code
Moisture-resistant drywall for bathroom and kitchen wet areas
All-purpose compound, topping compound, paper tape, and corner bead
Full hang of all walls and ceilings in remodel area
Full tape and finish to Level 4 standard, three coats with sanding
Apply specified texture style to match existing or per design
Dust containment barriers, HEPA vacuum, and final cleanup
Cut back damaged drywall to dry, sound material and remove debris
Inspect framing for mold, treat with antimicrobial if minor. Major mold requires specialist.
Mold-resistant purple board for replacement in water-prone areas
Install new drywall to replace water-damaged sections
Tape and finish seams between new and existing drywall
Match existing texture on repaired areas
Apply stain-blocking primer to prevent water stain bleed-through

terminologyGlossary.Type X: 5/8-inch fire-rated drywall required by code for garage ceilings and rated wall assemblies
terminologyGlossary.green board: Moisture-resistant drywall with green paper facing, for damp areas but not direct water contact
terminologyGlossary.purple board: Mold and moisture resistant drywall, suitable for bathrooms, basements, and laundry rooms
terminologyGlossary.butt joint: The seam where two non-tapered drywall edges meet, requiring more mud to finish flat
terminologyGlossary.tapered edge: The factory-recessed long edge of drywall that allows tape and mud to sit flush
terminologyGlossary.setting compound: Chemical-setting joint compound (hot mud) that hardens by reaction, not drying, for fast repairs
terminologyGlossary.all-purpose compound: Pre-mixed drywall mud suitable for bedding tape, filling, and finish coats
terminologyGlossary.topping compound: Lightweight finish compound for final coats, easier to sand than all-purpose
terminologyGlossary.Level 4 finish: Standard finish with tape and three coats of compound, suitable for flat or light texture paint
terminologyGlossary.Level 5 finish: Premium smooth finish with a skim coat over Level 4, required for gloss paint or critical lighting
terminologyGlossary.knockdown: Spray texture that is flattened with a knife while wet for a mottled, Mediterranean look
terminologyGlossary.orange peel: Light spray texture resembling the skin of an orange, common in modern construction
terminologyGlossary.skip trowel: Hand-applied texture using a curved knife to leave random raised areas
terminologyGlossary.corner bead: Reinforcing strip installed on outside drywall corners to create a clean, durable edge
terminologyGlossary.back blocking: Supporting a drywall joint from behind with a piece of plywood or drywall for butt joints
terminologyGlossary.resilient channel: Metal furring strip that decouples drywall from framing for sound isolation

seasonalTips.tip: Cold weather slows compound drying. Ensure heat is running in the workspace and allow extra dry time between coats.
Spring remodels are ramping up. Stock up on materials and book subcontractors early for new construction and renovations.
High humidity slows drying and can cause compound to sag. Run dehumidifiers and fans to maintain proper conditions.
Good conditions for interior work. Push drywall projects before the holiday slowdown. Ideal temperature and humidity for fast finishing.

followUpToneNotes: Drywall customers want to know their walls will look flawless when painted. Emphasize your finishing quality and the number of coats. Explain the difference between a Level 4 and Level 5 finish. Be upfront about the dust and timeline involved. Mention your dust control methods to put homeowners at ease.
